coredata.dc:title: Construction of a pH- and near-infrared irradiation-responsive nanoplatform for chemo-photothermal therapy
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Qiang
Gao, Jun
Ding, Chengqiang
Li, Shangji
Deng, Linhong
Kong, Yong
coredata.dc:description: Au nanoclusters, decorated with graphene quantum dots (GQDs), were obtained through photocatalytic reduction of AuCl4 3– by UV irradiation, and then cytarabine (Cyt) was loaded to the Au/GQDs via charge-dipole interactions. Mercaptopropionic acid (MPA) was anchored to the Cyt-loaded Au/GQDs through the formation of Au–S bond, which was further encapsulated by polyethyleneimine (PEI) via charge-dipole interactions. The delivery of Cyt from the quaternary complex (Au/GQDs/MPA/PEI) is pH-sensitive and can be modulated by near-infrared (NIR) irradiation. The results of cell viability test indicate that the developed nanoplatform can be used for chemo-photothermal combination therapy of cancer cells, and the efficacy of chemo-photothermal combination therapy is significantly higher than that of the single mode of photothermal therapy (PTT) or chemotherapy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanoplatform
Photocatalytic reduction
pH-Sensitivity
Near-infrared irradiation
Chemo-photothermal therapy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Evaluation of photothermal conversion ability of different samples
pH-sensitive and NIR modulated delivery of Cyt
Preparation of Cyt encapsulated quaternary complex
Confocal laser scanning images of Au/GQDs/Cyt/MPA/PEI
TEM images and EDS analysis of different samples
pH-sensitive delivery of Cyt from Au/GQDs/Cyt/MPA/PEI
NIR irradiation modulated delivery of Cyt from Au/GQDs/Cyt/MPA/PEI

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China
Advanced Catalysis and Green Manufacturing Collaborative Innovation Center, Changzhou University

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration showing the preparation of Au/GQDs/Cyt/MPA/PEI and controlled delivery of Cyt.
Determined zeta potentials of Au/GQDs, Cyt, MPA, Au/GQDs/Cyt/MPA, PEI and Au/GQDs/Cyt/MPA/PEI in water.
FT-IR spectra of Au/GQDs, MPA, Cyt and Au/GQDs/Cyt/MPA/PEI.
Confocal laser scanning images of Au nanoclusters (A), GQDs (B), Au/GQDs (C) and Au/GQDs/Cyt/MPA/PEI (D).
TEM images and EDS analysis of GQDs (A), Au nanoclusters (B), Au/GQDs (C) and Au/GQDs/Cyt/MPA/PEI (D).

(A) Photothermal conversion ability of GQDs, Au/GQDs and Au/GQDs/Cyt/MPA/PEI solutions (5 mL, 1.5 mg mL−1) under NIR irradiation. (B) Photothermal conversion ability of Au/GQDs solutions (5 mL) of different concentrations under NIR irradiation.

(A) Kinetic release curves of Cyt from Au/GQDs/Cyt/MPA/PEI in 0.1 M PBS of pH 5.0, 6.2 and 7.4, respectively, without NIR irradiation. (B) Kinetic release curves of Cyt from Au/GQDs/Cyt/MPA/PEI in 0.1 M PBS of pH 5.0 with and without NIR irradiation.
Cell viability of HL-60 cells treated with Au/GQDs/MPA/PEI and Au/GQDs/Cyt/MPA/PEI with and without NIR irradiation.

head.ce:title: Construction of a pH- and near-infrared irradiation-responsive nanoplatform for chemo-photothermal therapy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Radiology, Guangzhou First People’s Hospital, School of Medicine, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou 518000, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Radiology, Guangzhou First People’s Hospital, School of Medicine, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou 518000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Orthopedics, Changzhou Municipal Hospital of Traditional Chinese Medicine, Changzhou 213003, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Changzhou Municipal Hospital of Traditional Chinese Medicine

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Orthopedics, Changzhou Municipal Hospital of Traditional Chinese Medicine, Changzhou 213003, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Advanced Materials and Technology, Changzhou University, Changzhou 213164, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Advanced Materials and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Advanced Materials and Technology, Changzhou University, Changzhou 213164, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Au nanoclusters, decorated with graphene quantum dots (GQDs), were obtained through photocatalytic reduction of AuCl4 3– by UV irradiation, and then cytarabine (Cyt) was loaded to the Au/GQDs via charge-dipole interactions. Mercaptopropionic acid (MPA) was anchored to the Cyt-loaded Au/GQDs through the formation of Au–S bond, which was further encapsulated by polyethyleneimine (PEI) via charge-dipole interactions. The delivery of Cyt from the quaternary complex (Au/GQDs/MPA/PEI) is pH-sensitive and can be modulated by near-infrared (NIR) irradiation. The results of cell viability test indicate that the developed nanoplatform can be used for chemo-photothermal combination therapy of cancer cells, and the efficacy of chemo-photothermal combination therapy is significantly higher than that of the single mode of photothermal therapy (PTT) or chemotherapy.

ce:section.ce:para: As a traditional therapy for clinical cancer treatment, chemotherapy is often insufficient to ensure optimal success due to the high toxicity against normal cells and the drug resistance arising from tumor heterogeneity (Lim et al., 2019; Vankayala and Hwang, 2018; Sheng et al., 2020; Ji et al., 2017). Since the discovery of biologically transparent near-infrared (NIR) light (most around 800 nm), the past years have witnessed the establishment of photothermal therapy (PTT) as a complementary strategy for cancer therapy (Liu et al., 2019; Zhou et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2017; Tao et al., 2017). As a highly effective and noninvasive therapy, PTT is capable of delivering thermal energy focused to treated lesions (Korbelik et al., 2020). At present, nanomaterials such as carbon nanotubes (Marches et al., 2011), graphene (Yang et al., 2013); graphene oxide (Mauro et al., 2017) or reduced graphene oxide (Mauro et al., 2015); Au nanoparticles (AuNPs) (Li Volsi et al., 2016, 2017, 2018; Puleio et al., 2020); Ag nanoparticles (AgNPs) (Boca et al., 2011), CuS nanocrystals (Riedinger et al., 2015), and Pd nanosheets (Tang et al., 2011) have been widely used as photothermal agents to enhance the efficacy of PTT. Especially, AuNPs have obtained the most attention for medicinal applications due to their low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility as well as high photothermal conversion ability (Strong and West, 2015; Kennedy et al., 2011; You et al., 2010; Wang et al., 2019; Xia et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2019).

However, there are also disadvantages when single PTT mode is utilized for cancer therapy. The energy of near-infrared (NIR) is gradually reduced as it travels deeper into tissues due to light absorption and scattering, and thus some tumor cells would inevitably receive suboptimal laser exposure and may not be ablated (You et al., 2010; Shen et al., 2013). As an emerging clinical technique, chemo-photothermal combination therapy is particularly attractive. Besides the original superiority of PTT, hyperthermia can promote drug delivery into tumor and increase the drug toxicity (Shen et al., 2013). As a result, the efficacy of chemo-photothermal combination therapy is significantly higher than that of the single mode of PTT or chemotherapy.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as a popular zero-dimensional carbon material, have been widely used in the biomedical field, owing to their high solubility, convenience for surface grafting and easy preparation (bottom-up or top-down method) (Lv et al., 2016). Especially, the photocatalytic reduction ability of GQDs under UV irradiation has been demonstrated recently (Wu et al., 2016), providing the feasibility for the preparation of GQDs/metal NPs hybrids as the support for chemo-photothermal combination therapy.

Herein, we report on the facile preparation of GQDs decorated Au nanoclusters (Au/GQDs) via GQDs-assisted photocatalytic reduction of AuCl4 3– under UV irradiation. Cytarabine (Cyt), an anticancer drug, was loaded to the Au/GQDs via charge-dipole interactions. Mercaptopropionic acid (MPA) was then anchored to the Cyt-loaded Au/GQDs through Au–S bonds, which was further encapsulated by polyethyleneimine (PEI) via charge-dipole interactions. Next, the delivery of Cyt from the quaternary complex (Au/GQDs/MPA/PEI) was investigated, and the results indicated that the delivery of Cyt is pH-sensitive and can be modulated by NIR. Finally, the developed nanoplatform was evaluated by cell viability test, demonstrating its great potential for chemo-photothermal combination therapy of cancer cells.

Citric acid monohydrate (CA, analytical grade) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Chloroauric acid trihydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O), 3-mercaptopropionic acid (MPA, 98%), cytarabine (Cyt, 98%) and polyethyleneimine (PEI, Mw = 1800, 99%) were obtained from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Other reagents not mentioned were of analytical grade and used as received. Human leukemic (HL-60) cells and cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8) were obtained from EnoGene Biotech Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China). All aqueous solutions were prepared with ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ·cm, Millipore).

The zeta potentials and Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of different samples were measured by using a ZEN3600 zeta potential analyzer (Malvern, UK) and a Nicolet FTIR-8400S spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan), respectively. The in vitro fluorescence images of Au nanoclusters, GQDs, Au/GQDs and Cyt encapsulated Au/GQDs/MPA/PEI (Au/GQDs/Cyt/MPA/PEI) were recorded on a LSM-710 confocal laser scanning microsope (CLSM, Zeiss, Germany). The morphologies and element analysis of different samples were conducted with a JEM 2100 transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL, Japan) equipped with energy dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDS). The NIR irradiation was emitted from a FU808AD1000-F34 laser source (Shenzhen Zhonglai Technology Co., Ltd., China). The concentration of Cyt was measured by using a model U-3900 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). A microplate reader (Multiskan MK3, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) was used to determine the relative cell viability.

GQDs were synthesized by directly pyrolyzing CA which was used as the carbon source. 2.0 g of CA was dissolved in 15 mL of ultrapure water, which was then transferred into a crucible and heated at 175 °C in a ceramicfibremufflefurnace for 2 h to obtain the GQDs. After that, 0.5 g of the GQDs was dissolved in 25 mL of ultrapure water under sonication, and a yellow transparent GQDs solution (20 mg mL−1) was obtained.

2 mL of HAuCl4 solution (20 mg mL−1) was mixed with 2 mL of the above GQDs solution. After irradiation with UV light (365 nm, 20 W) for 5 min, the mixture was centrifuged and the obtained precipitates (Au/GQDs) were dried. In the preparation of Au/GQDs, the GQDs acted as both the stabilizer and the reducing agent. The obtained Au/GQDs are soluble in water, and 30 mg of the Au/GQDs was dissolved into 20 mL of ultrapure water, resulting the solution of Au/GQDs (1.5 mg mL−1). For control experiments, Au nanoclusters were prepared by washing the as-prepared Au/GQDs several times with water for the removal of the GQDs, centrifuging and drying.

10 g of Cyt was added into 5 mL of the above Au/GQDs solution. After continuous stirring for 12 h at room temperature, the mixture was centrifuged and the precipitates (Cyt loaded Au/GQDs) were re-dispersed in 3 mL of ultrapure water. After that, 0.1 mL of MPA was added into the above dispersion and magnetically stirred for another 12 h at room temperature, followed by the addition of 1 g PEI for the encapsulation of Cyt in the nanocarrier. For control experiments, Au/GQDs/MPA/PEI without the encapsulation of Cyt was also prepared by the same procedures except for the addition of Cyt. The encapsulation ratio of Cyt in the quaternary complex may be calculated according to the following equation: Encapsulation ratio (%) = (Mass of Cyt encapsulated/Mass of Cyt added) × 100.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of photothermal conversion ability of different samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the photothermal conversion ability of different samples, the solutions of GQDs, Au/GQDs and Au/GQDs/Cyt/MPA/PEI (5 mL, 1.5 mg mL−1) were placed into three beakers of 25 mL, respectively, and exposed to the NIR irradiation (808 nm, 1 W). Three mercurythermometers were placed vertically into the three solutions, and the temperatures of the three solutions were determined every 30 s.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH-sensitive and NIR modulated delivery of Cyt
ce:section.ce:para: 20 mg of the Au/GQDs/Cyt/MPA/PEI was filled into a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of 3500 Da, and then it was immersed into 30 mL of 0.1 M phosphate buffer saline (PBS) of pH 5.0, 6.2 and 7.4, respectively. The delivery of Cyt at different pHs was investigated at 37 °C with continuous stirring. To quantify the amount of Cyt released from the quaternary complex, 3 mL of the PBS was taken out every 1 h for UV analysis and an equal volume (3 mL) of fresh PBS was added to the solution for replenishment. The cumulative release of Cyt at time t was calculated by dividing the total amount of Cyt released at time t by the total amount of Cyt encapsulated in the Au/GQDs/MPA/PEI quaternary complex.

To investigate the NIR modulated delivery of Cyt, the dialysis bag filled with 20 mg of Au/GQDs/Cyt/MPA/PEI was immersed into 30 mL of 0.1 M PBS (pH = 5.0) at the initial temperature (T i) of 37 °C. After that, the delivery of Cyt was carried out with and without NIR irradiation (808 nm, 1 W), respectively, with continuous stirring. In the case of NIR irradiation, the solution was continuously irradiated for 12 h. The sampling procedure and the calculation of cumulative release were the same as those described above. Schematic illustration showing the preparation of Au/GQDs/Cyt/MPA/PEI and the controlled delivery of Cyt are presented in Fig. 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Cyt encapsulated quaternary complex
ce:section.ce:para: The role of charge-dipole interactions in the preparation of the Cyt encapsulated quaternary complex can be illustrated in Fig. 2 The zeta potential of Au/GQDs is − 18.3 mV due to the highly electronegative carboxyl (−COOH) groups of GQDs; on the contrary, Cyt is positively charged with a zeta potential of + 4.6 mV because of the protonation of its amino groups (−NH3 +). Therefore, Cyt can be easily loaded to the Au/GQDs via charge-dipole interactions. Due to the existence of − COOH groups, MPA is also negatively charged (−15.7 mV), which can be anchored to the Cyt-loaded Au/GQDs via Au–S bonds. The − NH2 groups of PEI can be protonated in water, resulting in positively charged PEI (+32.8 mV) and the subsequent charge-dipole interactions between the − NH3 + of PEI and the highly electronegative − COOH groups of MPA. Compared with PEI, the zeta potential of the Cyt encapsulated quaternary complex is greatly decreased to + 20.6 mV, which might be due to the notable diminishment of − NH3 + groups of PEI after their coupling with the − COOH groups of MPA. Noted that although the zeta potential of the Cyt encapsulated quaternary complex is greatly decreased to + 20.6 mV compared with PEI (+32.8 mV), it is still too positive. The relatively positive zeta potential of Au/GQDs/Cyt/MPA/PEI suggests that the amount of PEI containing − NH3 + groups in the quaternary complex is still considerable. As previously reported, PEI itself possesses slight degree of cytotoxicity (Wang et al., 2011), and therefore the quaternary complex could still be mildly cytotoxic.

The FT-IR spectra of different samples further confirm the successful preparation of the Au/GQDs/Cyt/MPA/PEI (Fig. 3 ). For Au/GQDs, the bands at 1716 and 1398 cm−1 are due to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching of C=O, respectively, from the –COOH groups of GQDs (Ding et al., 2020; Faolain et al., 2005). MPA has its characteristic bands at 1714 and 1398 cm−1 from the stretching of C=O, 2555 cm−1 from the stretching of S–H, and 689 cm−1 from the bending of C–S (Aswini et al., 2014). For Cyt, the band at 3440 cm−1 is due to the stretching of N–H (primary amine), and another two bands located at 1479 and 1109 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching of C=C and C–O, respectively. For the Au/GQDs/Cyt/MPA/PEI, two new bands at 2937 and 2839 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching of C–H from PEI (Linden et al., 2015), implying the successful encapsulation of Cyt in the carrier with PEI. It is noteworthy that all the characteristic bands except the one due to the S–H stretching (2555 cm−1) can be observed on the spectra of Au/GQDs/Cyt/MPA/PEI. The disappearance of S–H stretching might be ascribed to the fact that the S–H bond is replaced with Au–S bond when the MPA is anchored to the Cyt-loaded Au/GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Confocal laser scanning images of Au/GQDs/Cyt/MPA/PEI
ce:section.ce:para: The integration of Au nanoclusters and GQDs and the final encapsulation with PEI can be further confirmed by confocal microscopy, and the confocal images of Au nanoclusters, GQDs, Au/GQDs and Au/GQDs/Cyt/MPA/PEI are shown in Fig. 4 . The Au nanoclusters (Fig. 4A) and GQDs (Fig. 4B) emit green and blue fluorescence, respectively, at the excitation wavelengths of 430 nm (Au nanoclusters) and 405 nm (GQDs). For Au/GQDs, a blue-green fluorescence is observed under the successive excitations at 430 and 405 nm (Fig. 4C), suggesting the successful integration of Au nanoclusters and GQDs. After the encapsulation with PEI, the intensity of the blue-green fluorescence is a little increased (Fig. 4D), which could be ascribed to the introduction of additional − NH2 groups from PEI as the chromophore (Budzikiewicz et al., 2007).

ce:section.ce:section-title: TEM images and EDS analysis of different samples
ce:section.ce:para: The TEM images and EDS analysis of different samples are shown in Fig. 5 . The spherical GQDs exhibit a good monodispersity (Fig. 5A, left) with a narrow size distribution from 7 to 9 nm (Fig. S1A in Supporting Information), and the EDS analysis reveals that the GQDs are mainly composed of C and O elements (Fig. 5A, right). The Au nanoclusters are partially agglomerated (Fig. 5B, left), and the size distribution of the Au nanoclusters is significantly increased (160–200 nm) compared with GQDs (Fig. S1B in Supporting Information). Au and Cu elements are observed from the EDS (Fig. 5B, right), however, the Cu element of majority is from the Cu grid used as the support of EDS. As can be seen from Fig. 5C (left), the Au nanoclusters of dark color is completely wrapped in the GQDs shell of light color, confirming the successful integration of Au nanoclusters and GQDs. In addition, the EDS results also reveal the presence of C, O and Au elements in the as-prepared Au/GQDs (Fig. 5C, right). Compared with Au/GQDs, the thickness of the light colored shell of the Au/GQDs/Cyt/MPA/PEI is remarkably increased (Fig. 5D, left), which can be attributed to the anchoring of MPA to the Au/GQDs and the subsequent encapsulation with PEI. A new element, S, is observed for the Au/GQDs/Cyt/MPA/PEI besides C, O and Au (Fig. 5D, right). Obviously, the additional S element is from the S–H groups of the anchored MPA.

The photothermal conversion ability of GQDs, Au/GQDs and Au/GQDs/Cyt/MPA/PEI solutions (5 mL, 1.5 mg mL−1) under NIR irradiation is further investigated (Fig. 6 A). As can be seen, the temperature of the GQDs solution remains almost unchanged, suggesting poor photothermal conversion ability of GQDs. Excitingly, the temperature of the Au/GQDs solution increases rapidly from 25.0 to 47.6 °C upon irradiation with NIR for 5 min. The excellent photothermal conversion ability of Au/GQDs can be ascribed to the presence of Au nanoclusters, which can function as an outstanding photothermal agent. Similarly, the temperature of the Au/GQDs/Cyt/MPA/PEI solution can also increase rapidly from 25.0 to 45.9 °C owing to the presence of Au nanoclusters. Noted that the temperature increment caused by Au/GQDs/Cyt/MPA/PEI is a little smaller than that caused by Au/GQDs (1.7 °C), which might be due to the poor thermal conductivity of MPA and PEI. The ratio of the photo-thermal conversion efficiency of the complex to that of Au/GQDs can reach as high as 92.5%, indicating excellent photothermal conversion ability of the Cyt encapsulated quaternary complex.

Next, the concentration of Au/GQDs is optimized to obtain the highest photothermal conversion efficiency (Fig. 6B). Upon NIR irradiation for 8 min, the final temperature is gradually increased from 44.6 to 47.6 °C with increasing concentration of the Au/GQDs solutions (5 mL) from 0.5 to 1.5 mg mL−1. When the concentration is further increased from 1.5 to 2.0 mg mL−1, the final temperature is almost unchanged. That is to say, Au/GQDs of 1.5 mg mL−1 is enough to obtain the highest photothermal conversion efficiency. Therefore, 1.5 mg mL−1 is chosen as the concentration of the Au/GQDs solution for the preparation of Au/GQDs/Cyt/MPA/PEI in the present work.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH-sensitive delivery of Cyt from Au/GQDs/Cyt/MPA/PEI
ce:section.ce:para: According to the UV spectra of Cyt at different concentrations (Fig. S2A in Supporting Information), the standard curve of Cyt determined at 272 nm (Liu et al., 2017) is obtained (Fig. S2B in Supporting Information). After that, the encapsulation ratio of Cyt in the quaternary complex is calculated to be 68% according to the equation described above. The kinetic delivery of Cyt from the Au/GQDs/Cyt/MPA/PEI at different pHs (5.0, 6.2 and 7.4) at the T i of 37 °C is investigated, and the results are shown in Fig. 7 A. In the first 8 h, a quick delivery of Cyt occurs for the three tested pHs, and then the delivery gradually slows down and levels off at the 10th hour. Noted that the cumulative release of Cyt at pH 5.0 is remarkably higher that those at pH 6.2 and 7.4 (58.62% vs. 26.86% and 11.91%) at the 12th hour. Obviously, the delivery of Cyt from the quaternary complex is more pronounced in acidic medium. On one hand, the electronegativity of the –COOH groups of MPA is decreased in acidic medium, resulting in attenuated charge-dipole interactions between PEI and MPA and the consequently partial collapse of the quaternary complex. On the other hand, the electronegativity of the –COOH groups of GQDs is also lowered in acidic medium, which goes against the loading of Cyt to the Au/GQDs via charge-dipole interactions. Anyway, the pronounced delivery of Cyt in acidic medium is especially advantageous for targeted drug delivery in tumors because the extracellular pH in tumors is generally lower than that in normal cells (5.7–6.8 vs. ~ 7.4) (Webb et al., 2011).

ce:section.ce:section-title: NIR irradiation modulated delivery of Cyt from Au/GQDs/Cyt/MPA/PEI
ce:section.ce:para: The NIR irradiation modulated delivery behavior of the Au/GQDs/Cyt/MPA/PEI is also investigated at the T i of 37 °C at pH 5.0 (Fig. 7B). The rate of Cyt release is significantly increased with NIR irradiation, and the delivery of Cyt levels off quickly at the 6th hour. When the delivery of Cyt is carried out for 12 h with continuous NIR irradiation, the cumulative release is remarkably higher than that without NIR irradiation (78.64% vs. 58.62%). Obviously, the greatly enhanced cumulative release under NIR irradiation is due to the effective light-to-heat conversion ability of the quaternary complex, which facilitates the delivery of Cyt.

Finally, the application of the Au/GQDs/Cyt/MPA/PEI in chemo-photothermal combination therapy is assessed through cell viability test. The HL-60 cells are analyzed by conventional CCK-8 assays, and the results are shown in Fig. 8 . Without NIR irradiation, Au/GQDs/MPA/PEI has almost no effect on the growth of HL-60 cells at low concentrations (<10 μg mL−1), and more than 95% of the HL-60 cells are still alive. However, it exhibits a little cell growth inhibition at relatively high concentrations (50 μg mL−1, 85.9% cell viability), which might be ascribed to the slight degree of cytotoxicity of PEI (Wang et al., 2011). In fact, the relatively positive zeta potential of Au/GQDs/Cyt/MPA/PEI (+20.6 mV, Fig. 2) suggests that the amount of PEI containing –NH3 + groups in the quaternary complex is considerable, and thus the quaternary complex itself could still be mildly cytotoxic. Noted that the cell viability is remarkably decreased to 63.0% with 50 μg mL−1 of Au/GQDs/MPA/PEI under NIR irradiation for 10 min, which can be due to the efficacy of PTT resulted from the Au nanoclusters in the quaternary complex. The cell viability without NIR irradiation is only 20.8% after being treated with Au/GQDs/Cyt/MPA/PEI of the same concentration (50 μg mL−1), indicating the high efficacy of chemotherapy from Cyt. Excitingly, the cell viability is further decreased to only 3.0% upon NIR irradiation for 10 min, suggesting extremely high cell inhibitory ability of the Au/GQDs/Cyt/MPA/PEI. Compared with Au/GQDs/MPA/PEI, the excellent cell inhibition rate of Au/GQDs/Cyt/MPA/PEI can be attributed to the significantly enhanced efficacy of chemo-photothermal therapy. From the results of Fig. 8, it can be concluded that the efficacy of chemo-photothermal combination therapy is significantly higher than that of the single mode of PTT or chemotherapy. Compared with PTT- or chemotherapy-based cancer treatment previously reported, the as-prepared quaternary complex possesses the advantages from both PTT and chemotherapy, exhibiting higher cell inhibitory ability and efficacy.

To summarize, the Cyt encapsulated quaternary complex (Au/GQDs/Cyt/MPA/PEI) is facilely prepared via GQDs-assisted photocatalytic reduction and charge-dipole interactions. The Au nanoclusters in the quaternary complex exhibit excellent photothermal conversion ability under NIR irradiation. Meanwhile, the charge-dipole interactions between GQDs and Cyt and between MPA and PEI are influenced greatly by pH, resulting in pronounced delivery of Cyt from the quaternary complex in acidic medium. Therefore, the delivery of Cyt from the resultant Au/GQDs/Cyt/MPA/PEI is pH-sensitive and can be modulated by NIR irradiation. The results of cell viability test indicate that the developed nanoplatform can be used for chemo-photothermal combination therapy of tumor cells, and the efficacy of chemo-photothermal combination therapy is significantly higher than that of the single mode of PTT or chemotherapy.

Qiang Gao: Investigation, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Jun Gao: Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing. Chengqiang Ding: Investigation, Methodology. Shangji Li: Investigation, Methodology. Linhong Deng: Methodology. Yong Kong: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the financial support from Guangzhou Science and Technology Project of Health (20181A010009), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (11532003, 31670950), Primary Research & Developement Plan of Jiangsu Province (BE2019653) and Advanced Catalysis and Green Manufacturing Collaborative Innovation Center (ACGM2016-06-27).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijpharm.2020.120112.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Lim et al., 2019

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Self-assembled oxaliplatin(IV) prodrug-porphyrin conjugate for combinational photodynamic therapy and chemotherapy

sb:title.sb:maintitle: pH-sensitive drug delivery based on chitosan wrapped graphene quantum dots with enhanced fluorescent stability

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Sheng, W. Dai, J. Gao, H. Li, W. Tan, J. Wang, L. Deng, Y. Kong, pH-sensitive drug delivery based on chitosan wrapped graphene quantum dots with enhanced fluorescent stability, Mater. Sci. Eng. C 112 (2020) 110888.
